analogy: Think of "Democracy and Diversity" through the chapter's own opening images: G e n d e r , R e l i g i o n a n d C a s t e 29 Chapter 3 Gender, Religion and Caste Overview The existence of social diversity does not threaten democracy. Political expression of social differences... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Democracy and Diversity is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that g e n d e r , R e l i g i o n a n d C a s t e 29 Chapter 3 Gender, Religion and Caste Overview The existence of social diversity does not threaten democracy.

The text explains that political expression of social differences is possible and sometimes quite desirable in a democratic system.

The text explains that in this chapter we apply these ideas to the practice of democracy in India.

The text explains that we look at three kinds of social differences that can take the form of social divisions and inequalities.

The text explains that these are social differences based on gender, religion and caste.

The text explains that in each case we look at the nature of this division in India and how it gets expressed in politics.

The text explains that we also ask whether different expressions based on these differences are healthy or otherwise in a democracy.

The text explains that 30 D e m o c r a t i c P o l i t i c s Gender and politics Let us begin with gender division.

The text explains that this is a form of hierarchical social division seen everywhere, but is rarely recognised in the study of politics.

The text explains that the gender division tends to be understood as natural and unchangeable.

The text explains that however, it is not based on biology but on social expectations and stereotypes.

The text explains that public/private division Boys and girls are brought up to believe that the main responsibility of women is housework and bringing up children.

The text explains that this is reflected in a sexual division of labour in most families: women do all work inside the home such as cooking, cleaning, washing clothes, tailoring, looking after children, etc., and men do all the work outside the home.

The text explains that it is not that men cannot do housework; they simply think that it is for women to attend to these things.

The text explains that when these jobs are paid for, men are ready to take up these works.

The text explains that similarly, it is not that women do not work outside their home.

The text explains that 12:25 11:10 Daily time use (hours: minutes) Source: Government of India, Time Use Survey, 1998-99. + A ‘time use survey’ was conducted in six states of our country.

The text explains that women also do a lot of direct income generating work, but the bulk of their work is household related.

The text explains that but they drop out because parents prefer to spend their resources for their boys’ education rather than spending equally on their sons and daughters.  No wonder the proportion of women among the highly paid and valued jobs, is still very small.

The text explains that women in national parliaments in different regions of the world (in%) Note: Figures are for the per cent of women in the directly elected houses of parliament as on 1 October 2018.

The text explains that source: http://archive.ipu.org/wmn-e/world.htm Nordic countries Americas Europe Sub- Saharan Africa Pacific Arab States Region Asia 0 20 15 10 5 World Average 24 India 45 40 35 30 25 29.5 42.3 26.4 23.7 19.8 18.6 15.6 11.8

The text explains that population of different religious communities in India, 2011 Hindu 79.8 Muslim 14.2 Others 2% Christian 2.3 Sikh 1.7 Others include Buddhist 0.7% Jain 0.4% Other Religions and Persuasions 0.7% Religion not stated 0.2% Source: Census of India, 2011 In %

The text explains that g e n d e r , R e l i g i o n a n d C a s t e 41 Caste inequality today Caste is an important source of economic inequality because it regulates access to resources of various kinds.

The text explains that source: National Sample Survey Organisation (NSSO), Government of India, 55th Round, 1999–2000

The text explains that several political and non-political organisations have been demanding and agitating for an end to discrimination against particular castes, for more dignity and more access to land, resources and opportunities.

The text explains that it (a) prohibits discrimination on grounds of religion.

The text explains that (c) provides to all individuals freedom to profess any religion.

The text explains that (d) ensures equality of citizens within religious communities.

The text explains that social divisions based on _________ are peculiar to India.

The text explains that g e n d e r , R e l i g i o n a n d C a s t e 45 Exercises List I List II 1.

The text explains that a person who believes in equal rights and opportunities for women and men A.

The text explains that a person who says that religion is the principal basis of community B.

The text explains that a person who thinks that caste is the principal basis of community C.

The text explains that a person who does not discriminate others on the basis of religious beliefs D.

The text explains that castiest 1 2 3 4 (a) B C A D (b) B A D C (c) D C A B (d) C A B D 12.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Democratic Politics II, Chapter 3: Democracy and Diversity

prelims_traps: Do not treat (a) B C A D and (b) B A D C as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Democracy and Diversity; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Democracy and Diversity through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use (a) B C A D and (b) B A D C to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Democracy and Diversity, which statement is supported by the source text about Democracy and Diversity?
options: In villages, women fetch water, collect fuel and work in the fields.
In urban areas, poor women work as domestic helper in middle class homes, while middle class women work in offices.
In fact, the majority of women do some sort of paid work in addition to domestic labour.
But their work is not valued and does not get recognition.
